speaker: interviewer
text: Ms. Callwood, before we go on to post-war and your experience here in the United States, is there anything that you would like to discuss? That--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sontha, what-- what I remember most than anything during the war times is my personal emotions I have for Madam Rosie Rothschild. I feel that she became my mother and was my mother. And even though I have not seen and spoken with her for so many years, she will always remain my mother, my surrogate mother, who gave me a feeling of comfort and safe feeling, even though she herself was a victim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, she did not let herself known as being a victim. Rather, what can she do for the child to make that child feels safe? What things did she do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her presence, her approach in the way she spoke to a child. Just a knowledge of her strength, her spirits, and the courage of doing something that we knew we were protected by her. Because who else was protecting us, if not Madam Rothschild? She is the one who protected us. Nobody else did. Not-- at least not in that home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When a Gestapo came, they faced Madam Rothschild face to face. So we depended, 150%, on our safety and our survival for Madam Rothschild's courage, and integrity, and in her love for children. How could one not love such a woman? Did you miss her? When you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I missed her for many years, and I still do. More than anything, I would like to find her. I know she lived-- she came to the United States in 1948. She left Audergem and came to the United States with her husband and the two daughters, Miriam and Eva. Then she had a son in the United States by the name of Avram. They went then and migrated to Israel. There, the two daughters became schoolteachers. And the-- one of the daughters, at the time of her letter that she wrote to me, was already a mother. And she became, again, a lady with a home for children. She undertook it again to take care of children in Israel, in the city of Afula. And Madam Rothschild has dedicated all her life to children. That is a sacrifice, because her personal life was always engrossed with children all around her. Now, any person who takes the time to do this for 40 some-odd years, that's quite a person there. And I hope to find her before it's too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to the United States in, in June of 1953.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came by plane. It was not a jet, and it was the first flight in my life. Wow, wowee. That was heavy. But things changed, haven't they? The jets keep us moved. We came here. The Jewish Committee, again, helped us. They made sure that we are taken care of with clothing, with food. The first five days, we were brought to West 25th Street, New York City, in a hotel. We stayed there for five days, and then the Jewish Committee sent us to Detroit, Michigan. They placed us in a home. And again, we were given money, and food, and clothing, until my father can go on his feet. And then finally, we got our own apartment on Elmhurst Avenue by La Salle in Detroit, Michigan. And I started to work in a bookstore across from Wayne University. And I wanted so desperately to become a professional, but I could not afford it because I had to help my parents with financial support. After the bookstore experience, I worked for Bartons candy store-- I walked back and forth 15 blocks each way so I can save on the fare-- from-- from 5:00 to 11:00 at night. Ate a lot of truffles, though. Still love them. And then came-- my father realized while living in Detroit, Michigan that he could not cut his diamonds there. There's no-- like, you don't cut diamonds in Detroit. So he went by himself back to New York. And then when he'd settled in, he called for us. And believe me, it was painful for my mother, and my siblings, and myself to leave Detroit, Michigan because we had gotten accustomed to our first sofa in our life, our first bathroom in our life, our first lovely dining room in our life. So we decided we must go where the-- where Papa calls us. And so we went. And we stayed in a two-room apartment across the Wonderbread Factory in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, New York. And the kitchen was shared with the landlady. And boy, boy, boy. Two housewives together in the same kitchen, no way. That doesn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we then moved down to 345 Bradford Street, Brooklyn. And that was in the east New York area. As a matter of fact, Danny Kaye's old house-- when he was a young boy-- was across the street. I liked East New York in those days, in the '50s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did one more move to 623 Cleveland Street, deeper down in East New York, by Blake Avenue. And it is there that my romance with Tony began, my husband. I had a very active life in New York. I was lucky enough to advance the financial aspect of my life. If $75 a week in the mid '50s is a lot of money, then I was doing good. Of this, I will always contribute $20 every week to my parents. But I was responsible for my clothing, for my doctor bills, for my eyeglasses, for everything. I-- I took care of all my expenses. But I always contributed to the house. And the house was, I must say, in those days, far more peaceful than it used to be in the olden days. I guess when people grow up, they take better insights. People find more peace. And my father became, again, a diamond cutter. Worked on 47th Street, did magnificent work. My mother never worked. She was always a housewife. And I worked. Finally, when I met Tony in Greenwich Village, New York, I, I decided to go into the management business in show business. I had no experience. Only in Belgium, I did some plays for the school and so forth. I was always enamored in the show business world. And this is very funny. When Tony and I married, May 5, 1961, we decided in September of that year to move on to San Francisco. But I always liked green, green, green, and we said, let's go to Sausalito. But we knew that show business was not in that direction. We must go south. So we went to Los Angeles, and we lived in Hollywood. But prior to coming to Hollywood, while we were in San Francisco, I sent three telegrams. One to Frank Sinatra. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One to The Troubadour, and which one was the third one? I can't remember. And do you know that I got a response. And the-- not from Frank Sinatra-- I got a response. And because of that, Tony had a show in the Hermosa Beach. So you see, I do not know nothing, and I wanted to begin from the top. And thereafter, our travels took us-- two years into the marriage, we were already in Australia at the Chevron Hilton hotel. And Japan. We went to many countries of the world. We lived in-- in-- in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to Germany, the same country that has pained my life and my family's life so much, we went back. And there, Tony performed on German television with very, very high, wonderful reviews. So you see, the tides do turn. Life goes on. But we must always remember, the genocide happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1969. I wanted Tony to see the house I lived in after the war, and the house I lived in before the war. The house that I lived in before the war was standing, but the house that I lived in after the war had just been torn down a couple months ago, and the rubbles were still standing there. I wanted to show Tony the horrible condition in which I had to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm very lucky to be here. I eat well, I live well. I learned how to maintain health, because health is the most important thing. Without health, you can survive nothing. So life in America is very good, and I'm very appreciative. Do you? Do you ever have dreams or flashbacks or anything today?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a long time, Sontha, I could not look at Holocaust photos on television or in a movie. And I'm going to be very honest with you. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [CRYING] It's very difficult. [CRYING] When I went to see-- I-- I was able already to watch all kinds of films, becomes Tony calmed me down. He saw my screams, and my tears, and my nightmares. I, I got over it. But then I went to see [CRYING] Oh, God. This brought back everything. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was not one of the Schindler's List, but it was so well done, and so real, and so upfront. And this in itself is a-- how do you say this in English? A epitaph of what happened. And when I heard that-- oh, my God. My mind is going now. Tony, help me, please. You were speaking about

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your personal experience relate to And what parallels were you able to draw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because, number one, the starvation I went through. Molestation. The bombs. Not knowing when it will end, not knowing if I will live. Not seeing my mother and father. Not knowing who and where they were. Not knowing where anything was. And just going from day to day and saying, God, why am I alive? What is the purpose for my life? And I somehow groped in my brains to find a way, an excuse, to stay alive. And when I found the excuse, it became so easy. Because the excuse and the apologies for staying alive is so I could love Madam Rothschild another day, another day, because she was my mother. And when my mother, my real mother, came, I did not want to go to my family no more, because she became my real mother. And when I came back to my family, I went through eight years of tremendous pains because of the dysfunctional atmosphere in my family life. Both had suffered so much, and both brought their suffrage into the family life. This created a dysfunctional aura, and we children were in pain. And I tried to protect my mother and my brothers all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I saw the desperation of survival in I could not stop. I said, I stopped crying, I stopped crying. I could not stop crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my God. Every human being, Jew and non-Jew, has cried with that film. Because that film is one of the films that truly shows the spirit of survival, when all the spirits are taken from you, and you decided that those spirits are going to remain with you without them knowing, and you're going to survive it. That's why we, who survived, fought the element and did not kill ourselves. It was easier to kill ourselves and not to go through another day like that. But we survived because we saw some kind of light out there. Maybe it was the sun shining, maybe it was something there that inspired us to keep going. Maybe we wanted, subconsciously, to tell the story, as I'm doing today. We don't know, but we survived. And why we survived, I think in the very end a group, of Jews survived so that they could be the emblem, a stamp on paper that it really did happen. I let no one else say differently, because they're only fooling themself. And may their conscience go with them, because we lost our families, and our education, and our health, and our existence. We, who survive, will never be the same. We play, we laugh, we go to the theater. But down there is always that pain. We suffer every single day as survivors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we live harder than people who did not suffer, because we try to go this way and go this way. And that's what reminded me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what message or lesson do you have for the future generations and the world based on your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only philosophize in the best way I know how, and I realize one thing about human life. As long as we do not regard ourself as being better than another person, as long as we serve another human being with humility and respect and do not judge people by their color, by their religion, but only as two human beings and nothing more and nothing less, and treat each other with civil, we can live united on this Earth. No other way. That's my message. And I wish peace to everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you for sharing your story with us today. OK, this is Mr. Callwood with Mrs. Callwood. Tell us a little bit about yourself and your relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first I was born as a child. [LAUGH] And let me say this. To be serious about it, when I met this young lady here, I was working in Greenwich Village, New York in the coffee houses in the '50s, passing the hat around, during the era of folk music and poetry reading. And I met this young lady. And to make a long story short, I have never in my life have-- think that I would meet a person of this nature. Her philosophy, her, her ideals, her, her sensitivity, her beauty within, her spirituality. And I can go on, and on, and on, and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, well, this one, I'm not going to let go. This is going to be for life. And luckily, she accepted. And we have been sailing ever since. And I hope that it will last forever. How long will we be together now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, May 5th, we celebrate 34 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 34 Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we are in 1995, so you must know when we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What philosophy of life has kept you guys together so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, Sontha, I've always asked the purpose of being here on this Earth. And one day, when I was 42, I finally found my own personal answers. I cannot say, this is other people's thinking, but this is my thinking. Relativity, creativity, consistency. Relativity, in my estimation, is to be the total awareness of everything that emerges around you in whatever form it is, and in whatever movement it is. May it be the sky, or a table, or anything. Not to see just a table, but the creation of the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I discovered the insightness of the awareness, then why not give your creativity to the relativity so that your day becomes very fulfilled? Then why not consistently feed that of which makes your day so interesting and so fulfilling?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found even my second piece and second wind of why I want to live so much on this Earth. Because while we have the opportunity and the gift of life, we should live it in absolute joy, and share, and doing good for other human beings, and just think good. Drop the evil. Put it in a garbage can, put the lid on top. And this person here, I cannot say enough. He is the most beautiful, wonderful-- for a woman to be loved liked this, God gave me a great gift on Earth right here. And I cannot say enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we should wave the flag and, and, and keep the peace going. [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Sabina, you can tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I-- Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: About this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, certainly. The first name is Sabina is in the carriage. And this is my mother, Yetta. And she is pregnant with her son, Sammy. Wagschal, family name. The place is Antwerpen, on the street of Antwerpen. The date of the photo, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. How old-- I say you looked five and three, or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sabina?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or maybe four. But not more than four.

speaker: interviewee
text: First name, Samuel Aron and Sabina, last name, Wagschal. Brother, sister. Place, a park in Antwerpen area. Date of photo, 1940 or 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Does Stephen Spielberg know how hard you work?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHTER] OK, Sabina?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Salomon Wagschal, and my brother, Samuel Aron Wagschal, in a park in the Antwerpen surroundings. I believe this is circa 1939, 1940 also.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, Sabina.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is yours truly, Sabina Wagschal, around 1945, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo with us three-- Sam, Jack, Sabina Wagschal-- brother, brothers and sisters. Sammy is 10, Sabina is 11, and Jack is eight. This is after the war in Antwerp, Belgium. Next to it-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --This beautiful child in front of you is my sister, Sara-Golde Wagschal. I was a surrogate mama from birth to 11 and a half years, and this is in Antwerpen, Belgium. She's somewheres around 1953-- and '53-- or, I'm sorry-- '51. She looks like she's a little over year old there. She was born in 1950, so that's in '51. Antwerpen, Belgium. Sara-Golde Wagschal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my beautiful mama, Yetta Wagschal, at a place known as [NON-ENGLISH] where she was in a sanitarium, sent by the Belgian government, to recuperate from all her diseases and loss of weight so she could become again healthy, and alive, and do what she had to do. And she still looks beautiful. Circa 1946 over there on that photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Sabina. Sabina?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Yetta Wagschal, and my father, Salomon Wagschal. And this, I believe, is also in Brooklyn, New York, where they lived all their life besides Detroit, Michigan. They lived all their life in Brooklyn, where they also died. And I think this is somewhere in the middle '70s in the USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Sabina.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Antonio Callwood, also known as Tony St. Thomas. And we are celebrating here my 50th birthday in friend's homes. And God bless, our 25th anniversary there. So this is just wonderful, because now we are celebrating 34 years this year, 1995. Very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This here is my brother, Jack, on the cover of a thesis magazine that my niece, Nicole Wagschal, his daughter-- born and raised in the United States-- decided to do regarding the continued culture of Holocaust survivors. And she calls it the This is currently as an outline, but it will come out in its total thesis, and I will receive a total copy from her not too long thereafter. I think it deserves to be noticed. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
